ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (chicken meat dish) is the closest parent; no specific Mughlai Handi code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Mughlai Chicken Handi or a sufficiently close parent food concept.

main_ingredients: whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaf)
ground spices (coriander, cumin, turmeric, red chilli, garam masala)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Mughlai Chicken Handi is approximately 350 g, representing a single-person main course portion served in or from a clay handi pot, usually accompanied by naan or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken curry, with bone, cooked — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports chicken curry (bone-in, cooked) at approximately 150 kcal/100 g. Mughlai Chicken Handi is a richer preparation with cream, cashew paste, and ghee; values have been adapted upward to reflect the higher fat and energy content typical of Mughlai-style dishes, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for chicken curry (FDC ID 174972) and peer-reviewed compositional data for Mughlai-style chicken preparations. The adapted values represent a best-estimate composite for a restaurant-style Mughlai Chicken Handi.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat-based curry dishes — analogous estimate based on chicken curry GI data; University of Sydney GI Database reference for curry-type dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mughlai Chicken Handi. The University of Sydney GI Database lists chicken curry and similar spiced meat dishes in the low GI range (GI 30–45). A GI of 35 is assigned as a defensible analogous estimate for this high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 18.2) / 100 ≈ 6.4, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 5.2 × 3.5 = 18.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg/100 g; ~1470 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150–200 g and request reduced-salt preparation, as a full restaurant serving can contribute approximately 60–70% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and low net carbohydrate content (~5.2 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mughlai Chicken Handi has a low GI and low carbohydrate density, making it a suitable protein-rich main course for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or plain naan; pairing with cauliflower rice or a small portion of basmati rice is preferable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cream and ghee (~4.2 g/100 g) and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat from cream and ghee and the high sodium content of a full restaurant portion are relevant concerns for cardiovascular disease management; individuals should limit portion size, request preparation with less ghee and cream, and balance the meal with high-fibre vegetables to mitigate lipid and blood pressure effects.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mughlai Chicken Handi is a rich, slow-cooked Indian chicken dish rooted in the culinary traditions of the Mughal Empire, characterised by its preparation in a handi — a traditional deep, narrow-mouthed clay or metal pot that concentrates flavours through slow braising. The dish features bone-in or boneless chicken pieces marinated in yogurt and spices, then cooked with a sauce base of sautéed onions, tomatoes, ginger-garlic paste, whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaf), ground spices (coriander, cumin, turmeric, red chilli, garam masala), cream, and cashew paste. The result is a deeply aromatic, mildly spiced, creamy curry with a velvety texture and a rich golden-orange colour. The flavour profile is complex — warm, slightly sweet from the cashew and cream, with a gentle heat from the chillies and a fragrant spice depth from the whole spices. Nutritionally, Mughlai Chicken Handi is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with a low glycemic index (~35), making it suitable for blood glucose management when consumed in appropriate portions. The saturated fat from cream and ghee and the sodium content are the primary clinical considerations for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is naturally gluten-free and halal. In Singapore, this dish is served at Indian and Mughlai restaurants and is popular as a sharing main course accompanied by naan, roti, or basmati rice.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Mughlai Chicken Handi?
Is Mughlai Chicken Handi good for diabetics?
What spices are in Chicken Handi?

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN or USDA entry exists for Mughlai Chicken Handi as a named dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken curry data and USDA FoodData Central chicken curry entries, adjusted upward for the richer Mughlai preparation style (cream, cashew, ghee). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar curry-type dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation is grounded in two approved sources and the dish composition is well-documented.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN (chicken curry, cooked) and USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 174972, chicken curry) with upward adjustment for cream, cashew paste, and ghee typical of Mughlai Handi preparation. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 3. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 5. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Mughlai Chicken Handi was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. The input JSON listed cuisine as 'Turkish' and cuisine_tags as Turkish/Kebab/Shawarma, which appears to be a data error from the field survey source (the restaurant Sultan Palace serves both Turkish and Indian/Mughlai cuisine). The dish name 'Mughlai Chicken Handi' is unambiguously an Indian Mughlai dish; cuisine has been corrected to 'Indian (Mughlai)'. 7. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this dish is served in Singapore. 8. Serving size of 350 g is an estimate for a single restaurant main-course portion; actual restaurant portion may vary. 9. Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant context (Sultan Palace Turkish and Lebanese Cuisine, which serves halal food); this should be verified against the restaurant's halal certification.
